toolUseResult.newString:
setopt PROMPT_SUBST
PROMPT='${_PROMPT_DIR}${vcs_info_msg_0_} $ '
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# 基本設定
export LANG=ja_JP.UTF-8
export EDITOR=vim
export SAVEHIST=100000

# プロンプト設定（グラデーション付きディレクトリ + git ブランチ/ステータス）
zmodload zsh/mathfunc
zmodload zsh/datetime
autoload -Uz vcs_info add-zsh-hook

# 時間帯ベースの色相範囲（JST）
# 朝(5-9): 暖かい朝焼け, 昼(9-13): 明るい黄緑,
# 午後(13-17): 爽やかシアン, 夕方(17-20): 夕焼けマゼンタ, 夜(20-5): 深い青紫
_prompt_time_hue() {
  local hour_str
  TZ=Asia/Tokyo strftime -s hour_str '%H' $EPOCHSECONDS
  local -i hour=$(( 10#$hour_str ))
  local -i hue_min hue_max
  if (( hour >= 5 && hour < 9 )); then
    hue_min=15; hue_max=55       # 朝焼けのオレンジ〜ピンク
  elif (( hour >= 9 && hour < 13 )); then
    hue_min=55; hue_max=140      # 陽光の黄色〜緑
  elif (( hour >= 13 && hour < 17 )); then
    hue_min=140; hue_max=210     # 午後の緑〜シアン
  elif (( hour >= 17 && hour < 20 )); then
    hue_min=300; hue_max=360     # 夕焼けの赤〜マゼンタ
  else
    hue_min=220; hue_max=300     # 夜空の青〜紫
  fi
  _PROMPT_HUE=$(( hue_min + RANDOM % (hue_max - hue_min + 1) ))
}

# 初回の色相設定
_prompt_time_hue

# HSL の色相を truecolor エスケープ用 R;G;B に変換（S=70%, L=65% 固定）
_hue2rgb() {
  local -F h=$1 s=0.70 l=0.65
  local -F c=$(( (1.0 - fabs(2.0 * l - 1.0)) * s ))
  local -F hp=$(( h / 60.0 ))
  local -F x=$(( c * (1.0 - fabs(fmod(hp, 2.0) - 1.0)) ))
  local -F m=$(( l - c / 2.0 ))
  local -F r=0 g=0 b=0
  if   (( hp < 1 )); then r=$c; g=$x
  elif (( hp < 2 )); then r=$x; g=$c
  elif (( hp < 3 )); then g=$c; b=$x
  elif (( hp < 4 )); then g=$x; b=$c
  elif (( hp < 5 )); then r=$x; b=$c
  else                     r=$c; b=$x
  fi
  REPLY="$(( int((r+m)*255) ));$(( int((g+m)*255) ));$(( int((b+m)*255) ))"
}

# ディレクトリ文字列をグラデーションで着色
_gradient_dir() {
  local dir="${(%):-%~}"
  local -i len=${#dir}
  local -F step=$(( len > 1 ? 50.0 / len : 0 ))
  local esc=$'\e'
  _PROMPT_DIR=""
  for (( i = 1; i <= len; i++ )); do
    _hue2rgb $(( fmod(_PROMPT_HUE + (i-1) * step, 360.0) ))
    _PROMPT_DIR+="%{${esc}[38;2;${REPLY}m%}${dir[$i]}"
  done
  _PROMPT_DIR+="%f"
}

# vcs_info: git ブランチ名 + ステータス表示
zstyle ':vcs_info:*' enable git
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' check-for-changes true
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' stagedstr '%F{green}+'
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' unstagedstr '%F{yellow}!'
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' formats ' %F{cyan}(%b%c%u%m%F{cyan})%f'
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' actionformats ' %F{cyan}(%b%F{cyan}|%F{red}%a%c%u%m%F{cyan})%f'

# vcs_info hooks: ブランチ色分け + untracked 検出
zstyle ':vcs_info:git*+set-message:*' hooks git-branch-color git-untracked git-remote-status

# conventional branch prefix でブランチ名の色を変える
+vi-git-branch-color() {
  local branch="${hook_com[branch]}"
  local color
  case "$branch" in
    feat/*|feature/*)   color="%F{green}" ;;
    fix/*|bug/*)        color="%F{red}" ;;
    hotfix/*)           color="%F{magenta}" ;;
    docs/*)             color="%F{cyan}" ;;
    chore/*)            color="%F{yellow}" ;;
    refactor/*)         color="%F{blue}" ;;
    test/*)             color="%F{white}" ;;
    main|master|develop) color="%F{green}" ;;
    *)                  color="%F{blue}" ;;
  esac
  hook_com[branch]="${color}${branch}"
}

# untracked ファイルの検出（? 表示）
+vi-git-untracked() {
  if [[ $(command git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree 2>/dev/null) == 'true' ]] \
     && command git status --porcelain 2>/dev/null | command grep -q '^??'; then
    hook_com[misc]+='%F{red}?'
  fi
}

# リモートブランチとの差分（↑push待ち ↓pull待ち）
+vi-git-remote-status() {
  local -i ahead behind
  ahead=$(command git rev-list --count @{upstream}..HEAD 2>/dev/null) || return
  behind=$(command git rev-list --count HEAD..@{upstream} 2>/dev/null) || return
  local arrows=""
  (( ahead > 0 )) && arrows+="%F{green}↑${ahead}"
  (( behind > 0 )) && arrows+="%F{red}↓${behind}"
  [[ -n "$arrows" ]] && hook_com[misc]+="${arrows}"
}

_prompt_precmd() {
  _prompt_time_hue
  vcs_info
  _gradient_dir
}
add-zsh-hook precmd _prompt_precmd

# To Enable Ctrl+a, Ctrl+e
bindkey -e

# zplugの設定
source ~/.zplug/init.zsh

# 必要なプラグイン
zplug "zsh-users/zsh-completions"
zplug "zsh-users/zsh-history-substring-search"
zplug "Jxck/dotfiles", as:command, use:"bin/{histuniq,color}"
zplug "tcnksm/docker-alias", use:zshrc
zplug "k4rthik/git-cal", as:command, frozen:1
zplug "lib/clipboard", from:oh-my-zsh, if:"[[ $OSTYPE == *darwin* ]]"
zplug "jhawthorn/fzy", as:command, rename-to:fzy, hook-build:"make && sudo make install"
zplug "b4b4r07/enhancd", at:v1
zplug "mollifier/anyframe", at:4c23cb60
zplug "zsh-users/zsh-syntax-highlighting", defer:2
# git-prompt.zsh は不要（vcs_info でブランチ名を表示）
# zplug "~/.zsh", from:local

# プラグインのインストール
if ! zplug check --verbose; then
  printf "Install? [y/N]: "
  if read -q; then
    echo
    zplug install
  fi
fi

zplug load

# キーバインド設定
bindkey '\eOA' history-substring-search-up
bindkey '\eOB' history-substring-search-down
bindkey '^p' history-beginning-search-backward-end
bindkey '^n' history-beginning-search-forward-end
bindkey '^[[A' up-line-or-search
bindkey '^[[B' down-line-or-search

# 履歴検索の設定
autoload history-search-end
zle -N history-beginning-search-backward-end history-search-end
zle -N history-beginning-search-forward-end history-search-end

# Rust
if [ -d $HOME/.cargo ]; then
  source $HOME/.cargo/env
fi

# Aliases
if [ -f $HOME/.aliases ]; then
  . $HOME/.aliases
fi

# Python (pyenv)
if [ -d $HOME/.pyenv ]; then
  export PATH="$HOME/.pyenv/shims:$PATH"
fi

export PATH=${0:A:h}/bin:$PATH

# direnv
eval "$(direnv hook zsh)"

# SSH補完
function _ssh {
  compadd $(fgrep 'Host ' ~/.ssh/*.config | awk '{print $2}' | sort)
}

# poetry
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"


# Node.js (nodenv)
if [ -d $HOME/.nodenv ]; then
  export PATH="$HOME/.nodenv/bin:$PATH"
  eval "$(nodenv init -)"
fi

# atuin (シェル履歴管理)
if [ -f $HOME/.cargo/bin/atuin ]; then
  eval "$(atuin init zsh)"
fi

# パス設定
export PATH="/usr/local/opt/libpq/bin:$PATH"
export PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/mysql-client/bin:$PATH"
export PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/openjdk@17/bin:$PATH"

# jenv
export PATH="$HOME/.jenv/bin:$PATH"
eval "$(jenv init -)"


# SDKMAN
export SDKMAN_DIR="$HOME/.sdkman"
[[ -s "$HOME/.sdkman/bin/sdkman-init.sh" ]] && source "$HOME/.sdkman/bin/sdkman-init.sh"

# asdf
. /opt/homebrew/opt/asdf/libexec/asdf.sh

export DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH="$HOME/.local/lib:$DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH"

# opencode
export PATH="$HOME/.opencode/bin:$PATH"

# bun completions
[ -s "$HOME/.bun/_bun" ] && source "$HOME/.bun/_bun"

# bun
export BUN_INSTALL="$HOME/.bun"
export PATH="$BUN_INSTALL/bin:$PATH"

# Google Cloud SDK
source "/opt/homebrew/share/google-cloud-sdk/path.zsh.inc"
source "/opt/homebrew/share/google-cloud-sdk/completion.zsh.inc"

# claude-mem (memory service for Claude Code)
alias claude-mem="$BUN_INSTALL/bin/bun $HOME/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/thedotmack/plugin/scripts/worker-service.cjs"

file.content:
# 基本設定
export LANG=ja_JP.UTF-8
export EDITOR=vim
export SAVEHIST=100000

# プロンプト設定（グラデーション付きディレクトリ + git ブランチ/ステータス）
zmodload zsh/mathfunc
zmodload zsh/datetime
autoload -Uz vcs_info add-zsh-hook

# 時間帯ベースの色相範囲（JST）
# 朝(5-9): 暖かい朝焼け, 昼(9-13): 明るい黄緑,
# 午後(13-17): 爽やかシアン, 夕方(17-20): 夕焼けマゼンタ, 夜(20-5): 深い青紫
_prompt_time_hue() {
  local hour_str
  TZ=Asia/Tokyo strftime -s hour_str '%H' $EPOCHSECONDS
  local -i hour=$(( 10#$hour_str ))
  local -i hue_min hue_max
  if (( hour >= 5 && hour < 9 )); then
    hue_min=15; hue_max=55       # 朝焼けのオレンジ〜ピンク
  elif (( hour >= 9 && hour < 13 )); then
    hue_min=55; hue_max=140      # 陽光の黄色〜緑
  elif (( hour >= 13 && hour < 17 )); then
    hue_min=140; hue_max=210     # 午後の緑〜シアン
  elif (( hour >= 17 && hour < 20 )); then
    hue_min=300; hue_max=360     # 夕焼けの赤〜マゼンタ
  else
    hue_min=220; hue_max=300     # 夜空の青〜紫
  fi
  _PROMPT_HUE=$(( hue_min + RANDOM % (hue_max - hue_min + 1) ))
}

# 初回の色相設定
_prompt_time_hue

# HSL の色相を truecolor エスケープ用 R;G;B に変換（S=70%, L=65% 固定）
_hue2rgb() {
  local -F h=$1 s=0.70 l=0.65
  local -F c=$(( (1.0 - fabs(2.0 * l - 1.0)) * s ))
  local -F hp=$(( h / 60.0 ))
  local -F x=$(( c * (1.0 - fabs(fmod(hp, 2.0) - 1.0)) ))
  local -F m=$(( l - c / 2.0 ))
  local -F r=0 g=0 b=0
  if   (( hp < 1 )); then r=$c; g=$x
  elif (( hp < 2 )); then r=$x; g=$c
  elif (( hp < 3 )); then g=$c; b=$x
  elif (( hp < 4 )); then g=$x; b=$c
  elif (( hp < 5 )); then r=$x; b=$c
  else                     r=$c; b=$x
  fi
  REPLY="$(( int((r+m)*255) ));$(( int((g+m)*255) ));$(( int((b+m)*255) ))"
}

# ディレクトリ文字列をグラデーションで着色
_gradient_dir() {
  local dir="${(%):-%~}"
  local -i len=${#dir}
  local -F step=$(( len > 1 ? 50.0 / len : 0 ))
  local esc=$'\e'
  _PROMPT_DIR=""
  for (( i = 1; i <= len; i++ )); do
    _hue2rgb $(( fmod(_PROMPT_HUE + (i-1) * step, 360.0) ))
    _PROMPT_DIR+="%{${esc}[38;2;${REPLY}m%}${dir[$i]}"
  done
  _PROMPT_DIR+="%f"
}

# vcs_info: git ブランチ名 + ステータス表示
zstyle ':vcs_info:*' enable git
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' check-for-changes true
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' stagedstr '%F{green}+'
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' unstagedstr '%F{yellow}!'
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' formats ' %F{cyan}(%b%c%u%m%F{cyan})%f'
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' actionformats ' %F{cyan}(%b%F{cyan}|%F{red}%a%c%u%m%F{cyan})%f'

# vcs_info hooks: ブランチ色分け + untracked 検出
zstyle ':vcs_info:git*+set-message:*' hooks git-branch-color git-untracked git-remote-status

# conventional branch prefix でブランチ名の色を変える
+vi-git-branch-color() {
  local branch="${hook_com[branch]}"
  local color
  case "$branch" in
    feat/*|feature/*)   color="%F{green}" ;;
    fix/*|bug/*)        color="%F{red}" ;;
    hotfix/*)           color="%F{magenta}" ;;
    docs/*)             color="%F{cyan}" ;;
    chore/*)            color="%F{yellow}" ;;
    refactor/*)         color="%F{blue}" ;;
    test/*)             color="%F{white}" ;;
    main|master|develop) color="%F{green}" ;;
    *)                  color="%F{blue}" ;;
  esac
  hook_com[branch]="${color}${branch}"
}

# untracked ファイルの検出（? 表示）
+vi-git-untracked() {
  if [[ $(command git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree 2>/dev/null) == 'true' ]] \
     && command git status --porcelain 2>/dev/null | command grep -q '^??'; then
    hook_com[misc]+='%F{red}?'
  fi
}

# リモートブランチとの差分（↑push待ち ↓pull待ち）
+vi-git-remote-status() {
  local -i ahead behind
  ahead=$(command git rev-list --count @{upstream}..HEAD 2>/dev/null) || return
  behind=$(command git rev-list --count HEAD..@{upstream} 2>/dev/null) || return
  local arrows=""
  (( ahead > 0 )) && arrows+="%F{green}↑${ahead}"
  (( behind > 0 )) && arrows+="%F{red}↓${behind}"
  [[ -n "$arrows" ]] && hook_com[misc]+="${arrows}"
}

_prompt_precmd() {
  _prompt_time_hue
  vcs_info
  _gradient_dir
}

きれいになりました。残っている機能は:

1. **時間帯ベースのグラデーションカラー** (時間帯ごとに色味が変わり、範囲内でランダム)
2. **git ブランチ色分け + ステータス表示**
3. **リモートブランチ差分の ↑↓ 表示**

キラッと関連（`_hue2rgb_hsl`, `_hue2rgb_glow`, `_SPARKLE_*`, `TRAPUSR1`, バックグラウンドプロセス, クリーンアップ）は全て削除済みです。新しいタブで動作確認してみてください。

I found information about zsh animated prompt animation techniques using TRAPALRM and ZLE. Here's what the search revealed:

## Key Animation Techniques

The TRAPALRM trap can be used with `zle reset-prompt` to create in-place prompt updates. This is a fundamental approach for animating zsh prompts.

By setting `TMOUT=1` and defining `TRAPALRM() { zle reset-prompt }`, you can tell Zsh to regenerate your prompt every second. This won't cause you to lose any text you have already typed into your terminal.

## How It Works

The TMOUT mechanism is used to refresh the prompt in place every $TMOUT seconds by defining a callback TRAPALRM. The prompt itself (PS1) becomes animated, even when the shell is idle.

## Practical Example

An example implementation includes loading zsh modules with `zmodload -i zsh/datetime` and `zmodload -i zsh/stat`, then using TRAPALRM to update dynamic content. Setting PROMPT_SUBST option enables dynamic content evaluation in the prompt.

## Important Considerations

TRAPALRM should be created before you assign TMOUT to avoid a race condition where the signal arrives before it is trapped.

This technique is commonly used to create dynamic prompts with animated elements like timestamps, system information, or visual indicators that update periodically.

I found several relevant resources for terminal sparkle, glowing text animation, and shell effects:

## Key Tools and Projects

**TerminalTextEffects (TTE)**: TerminalTextEffects (TTE) is a terminal visual effects engine, application, and Python library. TerminalTextEffects is written in Python and does not require any 3rd party modules, uses standard ANSI terminal sequences, and should work in most modern terminals. It includes features for glowing text color effects.

**Termynal**: Termynal allows you to define prompt styles that specify the characters displayed before each line (like $ for command line inputs), with the option to customize their styling.

**Lolcat**: Lolcat is a utility that will accept stdin and paint whatever you pass it in a rainbow gradient effect. You can make lolcat animate the text by using the --animate flag.

## Shell Animation Creation

You can create animation characters using keyboard characters like -, /, or |, which can be static or moving on the screen, all involving shell scripting.

The search results show that TerminalTextEffects appears to be one of the most comprehensive tools for creating glowing, animated text effects in the terminal with extensive customization options.

The search results cover comparisons and integrations between these three popular shell prompts. Here's what I found:

## Overview of the Prompts

Starship is the minimal, blazing fast, and extremely customizable prompt for any shell. The advantage of Starship is that you can use it with Bash and Fish (Powerlevel10k is only for Zsh).

The advantage of Powerlevel10k is that it's much faster and has more features. Instant Prompt is one of the features that Powerlevel10k has but Starship does not.

Oh-my-posh was faster than powerlevel10k, which is pretty crazy.

## Animation and Animation Features

The search results show these are three competing modern prompt tools with different strengths—Starship offers broad shell compatibility, Powerlevel10k excels in speed and features, and Oh My Posh provides extensive theme options including Powerlevel10k-style variations.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings about zsh `reset-prompt`, flickering, copy-paste issues, and TRAPALRM problems:

## Main Issues with reset-prompt and TRAPALRM

Using `reset-prompt` to refresh the prompt at regular intervals causes text selection with the mouse to become deselected whenever the refresh occurs. This prevents copying text, as selected text will quickly become deselected.

The `menu-select` widget isn't prepared to have its display interrupted by `reset-prompt`, which can cause various UI issues.

## TRAPALRM Signal Handling

Creating TRAPALRM before assigning TMOUT helps avoid a race condition where the signal arrives before it is trapped. A common pattern used is:

```zsh
setopt PROMPT_SUBST
TMOUT=1
TRAPALRM() { zle reset-prompt }
```

However, this approach causes several problems:
- Arrow keys don't properly recall history - pressing up arrow once works, but pressing it again does nothing
- Tab completion navigation is broken with multi-line prompts

## Recommended Alternatives

The library `zsh-async` (written by Mathias Fredriksson) solves some issues with prompt updates and makes it easy to register async tasks and process results. This is a more robust approach than using TRAPALRM with frequent `reset-prompt` calls.

Here are the search results for "wezterm terminal text animation custom shader prompt effects":

## Key Findings:

**Custom Shader Effects (Feature Request)**
Some terminals have fixed shader effects like cool-retro-term, others like ghostty allow for shader effect programming on the user end, and users have requested the ability to affect the look of the terminal with custom programmable shader effects for things like retro scan lines, shaking text, animated backgrounds, and interactive visuals. However, as of the GitHub issue from May 2025, this appears to be a requested feature rather than an implemented one.

**Current Animation Capabilities**
The animation_fps setting controls the maximum frame rate used when rendering easing effects for blinking cursors, blinking text and visual bell, with larger values resulting in smoother easing effects but increased GPU utilization.

Animated GIF and PNG files will animate while the window has focus.

**Prompt Effects**
PromptInputLine activates an overlay to display a prompt and request a line of input from the user, and when the user enters the line, emits an event that allows you to act upon the input.

**Lua-Based Customization**
WezTerm's Lua configuration allows settings to be adjusted dynamically and portably across machines, and unlike terminals using data serialization formats like YAML or TOML, with Lua you can more easily achieve complex configs by leveraging dynamic scripts.

The search results show that while WezTerm supports animations and has powerful Lua-based configuration, full custom shader effects appear to still be in the feature request phase.

I've performed the web search for "zsh prompt ANSI escape sequence color cycling animation per-character". Here's what I found:

The search results provide information about using ANSI escape sequences with zsh prompts, though they don't contain specific examples of per-character color cycling animations.

**Key findings:**

1. **Basic ANSI Color Support in Zsh**: Zsh supports foreground color changes using escape sequences like %F, where the color can be specified numerically or by name in braces following the %F.

2. **Wrapping Non-Printable Characters**: Zsh allows you to include literal escape sequences within %{...%} braces, where the string inside should not change the cursor position and brace pairs can nest.

3. **Color Code Format**: The syntax uses %{\033[38;5;10m%} for 256-color codes, where the %{...%} syntax tells zsh to interpret ANSI codes.

4. **ANSI Escape Sequences Generally**: ANSI escape sequences are a standard for in-band signaling to control cursor location, color, font styling, and other options on video text terminals and terminal emulators.

However, the search results don't contain specific implementations for per-character color cycling animations. You would likely need to combine the above ANSI escape sequence knowledge with loop constructs or dynamic prompt generation to achieve this effect.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about terminal emulator custom shaders for glowing text effects in Kitty and Ghostty:

## Ghostty's Custom Shader Support

Ghostty features the ability to use custom shaders, which can be used for anything from creative looks and effects to accessibility features. Ghostty offers shaders—a feature you rarely see in terminals, allowing you to achieve custom glow or CRT effects with visual atmosphere that's both fun and functional.

### How Custom Shaders Work

Ghostty supports optional custom shader passes for user-defined visual effects, where each custom shader is run sequentially with the output of one shader feeding into the next. Shaders are written in the target graphics API's shading language (MSL for Metal, GLSL for OpenGL).

### Configuration Example

In your Ghostty config file, add a line like `custom-shader = /Users/<your-username>/.config/ghostty/shaders/amber-crt.glsl`, with the full absolute path working more reliably than the tilde shortcut.

### Recent Cursor Shader Enhancements

Custom shaders are now provided information about the terminal cursor for effects like trails or smears, with the community creating incredibly creative shaders beyond simple eye candy that can be practically useful too, such as making the cursor easier to follow.

## Kitty vs. Ghostty

Notably, while Kitty Terminal is known for speed and stability, it doesn't currently have custom shader background support like Ghostty does.

Based on the search results, I can provide you with information about how Powerlevel10k's transient prompt and instant prompt work internally:

## Transient Prompt

When Transient Prompt is enabled through p10k configure, Powerlevel10k will trim down every prompt when accepting a command line. The benefit is that transient prompt makes it much easier to copy-paste series of commands from the terminal scrollback by reducing screen clutter from previous prompts.

Internally, the transient prompt feature uses Zsh's line editor hooks. Based on the implementation details found in the search results, after accepting a command, the prompt is redrawn using a function that sets a flag variable to indicate the prompt should be shortened, then calls zle .reset-prompt to refresh the display.

## Instant Prompt

For Instant Prompt, instant prompt only speeds up zsh startup by making prompt appear quickly when you start zsh, before zsh is fully initialized. However, it has no effect on the speed of prompt after the first command.

The search results mention that for more detailed technical information about how Instant Prompt works internally, the Powerlevel10k documentation references "this section in zsh-bench," but the specific implementation details weren't included in the search results I found.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about TerminalTextEffects (TTE) integration with shell prompts in real-time:

## Overview
TerminalTextEffects (TTE) is a terminal visual effects engine that can be installed as a system application to produce effects in your terminal, or as a Python library to enable effects within your Python scripts/applications.

## Shell Prompt Integration
TTE includes a `--reuse-canvas` option that causes the terminal handler to move the cursor up by the number of visible lines in the input text, which will eliminate scrolling between effects. Additionally, the `--no-eol` option suppresses the newline that TTE normally prints when an effect ends to place the prompt on a new line below the canvas.

## Real-time Usage
For handling terminal setup/teardown, cursor positioning, and animation frame rate, a `terminal_output()` context manager is available. TTE offers two usage patterns: in CLI mode, pipe any text through TTE to instantly apply effects; in library mode, use the Python API for frame-by-frame control, ideal for scenarios requiring fine-grained customization.

## Example Integration
Users have successfully integrated TTE with shell prompts—for instance, some have configured startup animations with screenfetch at launch, using the beams animation and launching tmux.

Based on my search results, I found relevant information about working with readline and the line buffer. Here are some key techniques for creating animated shell prompts while respecting readline's buffer:

## Key Techniques for Animated Shell Prompts

**Using Readline Control Sequences:**
The `\1` and `\2` escapes begin and end sequences of non-printing characters, which can be used to embed a terminal control sequence into the mode string. This is important because it tells readline which characters are actual control codes vs. displayed characters, preventing buffer corruption.

**Preserving Buffer Integrity:**
When bracketed-paste is enabled, readline configures the terminal to insert each paste into the editing buffer as a single string of characters, instead of treating each character as if it had been read from the keyboard. Similarly, you should wrap ANSI escape sequences (animation codes) in these non-printing markers to prevent readline from miscounting line width.

**Buffer Redisplay:**
The search results reference buffer manipulation functions. You can change what's displayed on the screen to reflect the current contents of the readline line buffer. This allows you to update animations without corrupting what the user is typing.

**General Approach:**
For shell prompts specifically, wrap animation sequences in `\[` and `\]` (bash) or their readline equivalents to tell readline these characters take zero display width. This prevents the prompt from being miscalculated, which would corrupt the input buffer position.

The search results didn't provide specific code examples for animated prompts, so you may want to consult the GNU Readline Library documentation directly for advanced prompt animation techniques.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found regarding Starship prompt customization with custom animations and dynamic color changes:

## Key Findings

**Custom Modules and Color Customization:**

You can insert your own functions into the prompt-rendering procedure by running a custom function right before the prompt is drawn, which enables dynamic behavior per render.

**Dynamic Color Changes:**

You can dynamically change the color palette on each render using shell commands that select from available palettes. This is demonstrated through examples that randomly select from configured palettes.

**Custom Modules:**

Custom modules can be used to display desirable icons in the prompt with the possibilities being endless. You can create your own custom color palette in ~/.config/starship.toml to manipulate colors easily.

**Custom Animation Approach:**

While Starship doesn't have native animation support built-in, you can use the starship_precmd_user_func in bash to define custom functions that execute right before the prompt is drawn, allowing you to implement animation effects through changing output on each render.

The approach involves combining custom shell functions with Starship's configuration system to achieve dynamic, per-render color changes and animation-like effects. The key is leveraging the precmd hooks available in various shells (bash, zsh, fish, etc.) to update colors or content before each prompt render.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about combining zsh prompt customization with TMOUT and TRAPALRM for animated effects.

## Key Findings

**TMOUT and TRAPALRM for Animated Prompts:**

Zsh's built-in TMOUT mechanism can be co-opted with a TRAPALRM callback to refresh the prompt in place every $TMOUT seconds. The feature seems to be built for security purposes (e.g. logging you out if you take too long to input a command), but it can be repurposed for prompt animation.

A practical example from the results shows:
```
TMOUT=1
TRAPALRM() { zle reset-prompt }
```

This pattern allows prompt updates without blocking user interaction.

**Rainbow/Color Gradient Prompts:**

The search results indicate that there are ZSH themes which introduce rainbow-gradient prompts, though specific implementation details for safe color cycling weren't extensively covered in these results.

**precmd Hook Usage:**

The precmd hook executes before each prompt is displayed and can be used to run custom commands or functions. This is commonly used in combination with prompt features for dynamic updates.

However, the search results don't contain specific 2024-2025 documentation about safe implementations combining all these techniques (TRAPALRM, TMOUT, animated color gradients, and cycling rainbow effects). For the most current best practices, you may want to check recent GitHub repositories or zsh-specific forums.

Based on the web search results, I found information about Ghostty custom shaders and glow effects, though the search results don't contain specific details about applying glow effects to specific characters or prompt lines in GLSL.

Here's what I found:

## Ghostty Custom Shaders Overview

To enable a custom shader in Ghostty, you copy your chosen .glsl file to `~/.config/ghostty/shaders/shader.glsl` and add `custom-shader = ~/.config/ghostty/shaders/shader.glsl` to your Ghostty config file.

## Shader Features Available

Custom shaders are provided information about the terminal cursor, so that custom effects and animations can be applied to it, like a trail or smear. Additionally, custom shaders can now be hot reloaded.

## Glow Effects in Community Shaders

There are community shader repositories that implement glow effects. Some GLSL shaders give your Ghostty terminal a classic CRT look, featuring Amber & Green & Blue Phosphor Glow, Soft Background Glow, and Subtle Scanlines & Flicker. Another example is a shader that "adds chromatic aberration, glow, scanlines and dot matrix for an authentic CRT look".

## Shadertoy Compatibility

Ghostty exposes the Shadertoy interface, so you can take many Shadertoy shaders and drop them into Ghostty without any modification, and you can use Shadertoy itself as a live, interactive development tool for Ghostty custom shaders.

For creating a shader that applies glow specifically to certain characters or the prompt line, you would likely need to write custom GLSL code that analyzes the texture coordinates or character positions to selectively apply glow effects. The search results don't contain specific implementation details for this use case.

Based on the search results, I found several issues related to `zle reset-prompt`, though the specific combination you're searching for (text selection/copy-paste mouse issues) doesn't appear prominently in these results. However, I can provide you with relevant information about `zle reset-prompt` problems and workarounds:

## General Issues with `zle reset-prompt`

When `zle reset-prompt` is used aggressively (e.g., in a precmd hook that runs on every prompt), it can interfere with ZLE's internal state during menu completion.

## Recommended Workarounds

Only use `reset-prompt` when necessary (e.g., after manual prompt edits), and prefer `zle -R` for lightweight redraws.

The root fix is updating to Zsh ≥5.8.1, where state synchronization between reset-prompt and completion is patched. For older systems, workarounds like `zle -R` or debouncing reset-prompt calls can mitigate issues.

## Additional Considerations

For real-time data (e.g., live Git status), use background jobs instead of precmd. Tools like starship or powerlevel10k (with P10K_INSTANT_PROMPT=quiet) minimize reset-prompt usage.

The search results focus primarily on completion menu issues and prompt display problems rather than mouse selection/copy-paste specifically. If you're experiencing mouse-related issues specifically, you may want to check your terminal's mouse support settings or search for issues specific to your terminal emulator in combination with zsh.

Based on the web search results, I found relevant information about the kitty terminal text selection and `reset-prompt` conflict with zsh.

## Summary

The issue involves zsh configurations that use `reset-prompt` to refresh the prompt at regular intervals, which causes any text selected with the mouse in the kitty terminal to become deselected. This problem does not occur in other terminal applications like the default macOS terminal application, iTerm, or alacritty when using the same zsh config.

This issue is documented in GitHub issue #7408 on the kovidgoyal/kitty repository (dated May 2, 2024), indicating it's a known problem specific to how kitty handles terminal selection state during prompt refreshes in zsh.

The search results show this is a recognized compatibility issue, though specific workarounds aren't detailed in the top search results. The issue appears to be related to how kitty manages text selection state when zsh shell integration features trigger prompt resets.

I found comprehensive information about asynchronous Zsh Git prompts and state management issues. Here are the key findings:

## Asynchronous Zsh Git Prompts

Since asynchronous prompts work with delayed Git status updates, the prompt remains responsive even for huge repositories or slow disks.

Several popular solutions address this:

1. **zsh-async Library**: If the async process buffer becomes corrupt, the callback is invoked with a non-zero return code; a corrupted file descriptor is a known issue that can cause async worker crashes.

2. **State Management Issues**: Before spawning a new subprocess, the system checks if a previous subprocess is still running and closes it if the file descriptor is still open. It's recommended to delay prompt updates until the buffer is empty to prevent strange states in ZLE.

3. **Prompt Rendering**: The effect of asynchronous prompts is that they render right away, and then (sometimes) change a half second later.

## Flicker and Corruption Prevention

When the subprocess completes and writes output, a ZLE file descriptor handler triggers a callback that reads output and triggers a prompt repaint if the output has changed. Some prompts keep the previous prompt displayed while the new prompt is being rendered to avoid flickering.

## Workarounds

If async versions break, users can switch to synchronous git status by setting `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt false`.

content.text:
Now I have comprehensive findings. Let me compile the thorough report.

---

## Comprehensive Report: Animated Shell Prompt Techniques in Zsh/Terminal

### 1. The Core Mechanism: TRAPALRM + `zle reset-prompt`

The most widely documented technique for animating a zsh prompt is combining `TMOUT` with a `TRAPALRM` trap:

```zsh
setopt PROMPT_SUBST
TMOUT=1
TRAPALRM() { zle reset-prompt }
```

This causes zsh to re-evaluate and redraw the prompt every `$TMOUT` seconds. Because `PROMPT_SUBST` is enabled, any shell functions or variables referenced in `PS1` are re-evaluated on each redraw. This is the foundation used by animated clocks, CPU charts, and color-cycling prompts.

**Critical limitation**: The maximum granularity is 1 second (TMOUT is integer-only). You cannot get sub-second animation frames with this approach. True "sparkle" or "sweeping light" effects require much higher frame rates (10-30 fps) which this mechanism cannot provide.

### 2. Known Problems with `zle reset-prompt` (The Dealbreaker List)

This is the central problem. Repeated calls to `zle reset-prompt` cause a cascade of well-documented issues:

- **Text selection breaks**: In Kitty terminal, `reset-prompt` causes any mouse-selected text to become deselected on every refresh. This is documented in [Kitty issue #7408](https://github.com/kovidgoyal/kitty/issues/7408). The maintainer (Kovid Goyal) acknowledged it. Other terminals like iTerm2 and Alacritty reportedly handle this better, but it is fundamentally fragile across the board.

- **Tab completion corruption**: Multi-line prompts combined with `reset-prompt` break tab completion navigation. The `menu-select` widget is not designed to be interrupted by prompt resets. Documented in [Oh My Zsh issue #7393](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/7393).

- **History navigation breaks**: Pressing the up arrow to recall history works once, but a second press does nothing because the TRAPALRM fires and resets state. Documented in [Oh My Zsh issue #5910](https://github.com/robbyrussell/oh-my-zsh/issues/5910).

- **Right-aligned prompt corruption**: RPROMPT breaks on reset-prompt in some configurations. Documented in [Oh My Posh issue #6430](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6430).

- **Extra blank lines in iTerm2**: `zle reset-prompt` creates additional empty lines. Documented in [iTerm2 issue #8822](https://gitlab.com/gnachman/iterm2/-/issues/8822).

**Recommended mitigations** (partial, not complete fixes):
- Use `zle -R` instead of `zle reset-prompt` for lightweight redraws (but this does not re-evaluate `$PS1`).
- Upgrade to Zsh >= 5.8.1 which patches some state synchronization between `reset-prompt` and completion.
- Debounce `reset-prompt` calls.

### 3. Popular Prompt Frameworks: No Animation Support

**Starship** (Rust-based, cross-shell):
- No built-in animation or frame-based rendering.
- Supports dynamic color via `precmd` hooks, but only changes color once per prompt render (i.e., when you press Enter).
- You could theoretically use `starship_precmd_user_func` to cycle colors on each render, but this is static between commands, not animated.

**Powerlevel10k** (zsh-only):
- No animation support. Its design philosophy is the opposite: Instant Prompt renders the prompt as fast as possible (before zsh finishes initializing) and Transient Prompt simplifies old prompts to reduce clutter.
- Internally uses `zle .reset-prompt` but only at specific lifecycle points (command acceptance), not in a loop.
- Explicitly optimized to minimize `reset-prompt` calls, not increase them.

**Oh My Posh** (Go-based, cross-shell):
- Has extensive theming with gradients, icons, and segment styling.
- Has "transient prompt" support.
- No animation or frame-based rendering between command entries.

**Conclusion**: None of the three major prompt frameworks support or plan to support prompt animation. They all treat the prompt as a static render that is computed once per command cycle.

### 4. Terminal-Emulator-Level Effects (The Most Promising Avenue)

**Ghostty** (Zig-based terminal):
- Supports **custom GLSL shaders** that are applied as post-processing passes over the entire terminal framebuffer.
- Shaders have access to cursor position information, enabling effects like cursor trails and smears.
- Exposes the **Shadertoy interface**, meaning many existing Shadertoy shaders work directly.
- Community has created CRT glow shaders, phosphor effects, scanline effects, and chromatic aberration.
- Shaders can be **hot-reloaded**.
- **Key limitation**: Shaders operate on the entire screen, not on specific text ranges. A glow shader makes ALL text glow, not just the prompt. There is no mechanism to say "apply this effect only to the prompt line" because the shader operates on rendered pixels, not on semantic terminal content.

**WezTerm**:
- Has `animation_fps` config for controlling cursor blink and visual bell animation frame rate.
- Supports animated GIF/PNG backgrounds.
- Has a [feature request for custom GPU shader effects](https://github.com/wezterm/wezterm/issues/6985) but this is NOT implemented as of the latest information.
- Rich Lua scripting API but no per-cell visual effects or text glow API.

**Kitty**:
- Does NOT support custom shaders. A [feature request exists](https://github.com/kovidgoyal/kitty/issues/8250) but was not accepted.

### 5. External Tools: TerminalTextEffects (TTE)

[TerminalTextEffects](https://github.com/ChrisBuilds/terminaltexteffects) is a Python-based terminal visual effects engine that produces stunning effects (sparkle, beams, waves, rain, etc.) using standard ANSI escape sequences. It includes:
- `--reuse-canvas` option to prevent scrolling between effect frames.
- `--no-eol` to suppress trailing newlines.
- A Python library API for frame-by-frame control.

**However**: TTE is designed for one-shot text transformations (pipe text in, watch the effect, then it is done). It is NOT designed to be continuously running on a live, interactive prompt line. Using it on a prompt would require:
1. Capturing the prompt text.
2. Running TTE's animation loop (which takes over cursor positioning).
3. Restoring the prompt and readline state afterward.

This fundamentally conflicts with zsh's line editor (ZLE). TTE's animation loop would block user input, corrupt the readline buffer position, and break all interactive features. It is suitable for shell startup splash screens (e.g., animating a motd or screenfetch output) but not for the active prompt line.

### 6. Rust/Go CLI Animation Tools

- **cli-animate** (Rust crate): Provides spinner animations, progress bars, and menu animations for CLI apps. Not designed for shell prompt integration.
- **asky**, **inquire**, **promkit** (Rust): Interactive prompt libraries for Rust CLI applications (like Python's `inquire`). These create their own prompt UI; they do not integrate with zsh's prompt.

None of these tools provide a mechanism to animate the zsh prompt line itself.

### 7. The Async Prompt Approach (Best Current Practice for Dynamic Prompts)

The most robust approach for dynamic prompt content (not true animation, but "updates when data arrives"):

- **zsh-async** ([github.com/mafredri/zsh-async](https://github.com/mafredri/zsh-async)): Spawns background workers, and when results arrive, triggers a single `reset-prompt` to update the prompt. Used by the `pure` prompt and others.
- **git-prompt.zsh** ([github.com/woefe/git-prompt.zsh](https://github.com/woefe/git-prompt.zsh)): Asynchronous git status that renders immediately with a placeholder, then updates when git data is available.
- **agkozak-zsh-prompt**: Fast async prompt with careful state management to prevent flicker.

These approaches work well because they call `reset-prompt` infrequently (only when new data arrives), which minimizes the copy/paste and completion breakage. But they are not "animations" -- they are one-time deferred updates.

### 8. Creative Hacks (Theoretical, Not Production-Ready)

**Background process writing ANSI sequences directly**:
You could theoretically have a background process that uses ANSI cursor-positioning escape sequences (`\033[<row>;<col>H`) to overwrite specific characters on the prompt line with different colors in a loop. This would bypass ZLE entirely.

Problems:
- Races with ZLE's own screen updates.
- Any user keypress triggers ZLE to redraw, potentially leaving artifacts.
- Terminal resize would corrupt everything.
- Copy/paste would capture the raw escape sequences or garbled intermediate states.
- No way to coordinate with ZLE's understanding of the screen.

**Wezterm Lua event-driven approach**:
Wezterm's `format-tab-title` and `format-window-title` events can be used to animate tab titles and window titles with Lua callbacks. However, there is no equivalent event for "format the prompt line content." The terminal emulator does not have semantic awareness of which text on screen is the shell prompt.

---

## Final Answer

**Is there any known technique that allows animated text effects on the active prompt line WITHOUT breaking copy/paste and WITHOUT corrupting the prompt state?**

**No. There is no known reliable technique.** Here is why:

1. **The zsh approach (`TRAPALRM` + `zle reset-prompt`)** is the closest thing that exists, but it is limited to 1-second granularity (far too slow for sparkle/glow effects), and it breaks text selection, tab completion, history navigation, and right-prompt rendering across multiple terminal emulators. These are not edge cases -- they are well-documented, reproducible bugs filed against Kitty, Oh My Zsh, Oh My Posh, and iTerm2.

2. **Terminal-emulator shaders (Ghostty)** can add global glow/CRT effects to ALL terminal text, but cannot target only the prompt line. This is the closest to "working" animated effects, but it is whole-screen, not prompt-specific, and requires Ghostty (not Wezterm, Kitty, or iTerm2).

3. **No major prompt framework** (Starship, Powerlevel10k, Oh My Posh) supports or has plans to support prompt-line animation. Their architectures are built around computing the prompt once per command cycle.

4. **External tools** (TTE, cli-animate) produce beautiful terminal animations but are fundamentally incompatible with a live interactive prompt because they take over cursor positioning and block user input.

5. **The fundamental architectural barrier** is that shell line editors (ZLE in zsh, readline in bash) assume they have exclusive control over the prompt line's display. Any external process or timer that modifies those screen cells creates a race condition with the line editor. There is no coordination protocol between "something animating the prompt" and "the line editor tracking cursor position, text selection, and completion menus."

**What IS practically possible**:

- **Per-command color cycling**: Change the prompt's color scheme each time a command is executed (via `precmd`). This is safe, looks dynamic, and breaks nothing. The [rainbow-zsh](https://github.com/landoncrabtree/rainbow-zsh) theme does this.
- **Ghostty global glow shader**: Apply a CRT/glow/phosphor shader to all terminal text. This gives a "glowing prompt" effect as a side effect of all text glowing. Safe, no prompt corruption, but affects everything.
- **Startup animation**: Use TTE to animate your motd/welcome message when a new shell starts, then transition to a static prompt. This is safe because no interactive editing is happening during the animation.
- **Transient prompt**: Use Powerlevel10k or Oh My Posh transient prompt to simplify old prompts after command execution, creating a dynamic visual effect without animation.

Sources:
- [Animated CPU chart in the Zsh prompt](https://coderwall.com/p/k-ocpw/animated-cpu-chart-in-the-zsh-prompt)
- [Zsh Mailing List: Constantly updated clock in prompt](https://www.zsh.org/mla/users/2013/msg00687.html)
- [Text selection with zsh reset-prompt - Kitty issue #7408](https://github.com/kovidgoyal/kitty/issues/7408)
- [zle reset-prompt breaks tab completion - Oh My Zsh issue #7393](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/7393)
- [Arrow keys broken with reset-prompt - Oh My Zsh issue #5910](https://github.com/robbyrussell/oh-my-zsh/issues/5910)
- [Right-aligned prompt breaks on reset-prompt - Oh My Posh issue #6430](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6430)
- [How to write an asynchronous Zsh prompt](https://medium.com/@henrebotha/how-to-write-an-asynchronous-zsh-prompt-b53e81720d32)
- [TerminalTextEffects (TTE)](https://github.com/ChrisBuilds/terminaltexteffects)
- [TTE on Hacker News](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=40503202)
- [Ghostty GPU Shader Effects issue #6985](https://github.com/wezterm/wezterm/issues/6985)

- [Retro CRT Glow for Ghostty](https://thijskok.nl/retro-vibes-crt-glow-for-your-ghostty-terminal/)

- Shaders have access to cursor position information, enabling effects like cursor trails and smears.
- Exposes the **Shadertoy interface**, meaning many existing Shadertoy shaders work directly.
- Community has created CRT glow shaders, phosphor effects, scanline effects, and chromatic aberration.
- Shaders can be **hot-reloaded**.
- **Key limitation**: Shaders operate on the entire screen, not on specific text ranges. A glow shader makes ALL text glow, not just the prompt. There is no mechanism to say "apply this effect only to the prompt line" because the shader operates on rendered pixels, not on semantic terminal content.

**WezTerm**:
- Has `animation_fps` config for controlling cursor blink and visual bell animation frame rate.
- Supports animated GIF/PNG backgrounds.
- Has a [feature request for custom GPU shader effects](https://github.com/wezterm/wezterm/issues/6985) but this is NOT implemented as of the latest information.
- Rich Lua scripting API but no per-cell visual effects or text glow API.

**Kitty**:
- Does NOT support custom shaders. A [feature request exists](https://github.com/kovidgoyal/kitty/issues/8250) but was not accepted.

### 5. External Tools: TerminalTextEffects (TTE)

[TerminalTextEffects](https://github.com/ChrisBuilds/terminaltexteffects) is a Python-based terminal visual effects engine that produces stunning effects (sparkle, beams, waves, rain, etc.) using standard ANSI escape sequences. It includes:
- `--reuse-canvas` option to prevent scrolling between effect frames.
- `--no-eol` to suppress trailing newlines.
- A Python library API for frame-by-frame control.

**However**: TTE is designed for one-shot text transformations (pipe text in, watch the effect, then it is done). It is NOT designed to be continuously running on a live, interactive prompt line. Using it on a prompt would require:
1. Capturing the prompt text.
2. Running TTE's animation loop (which takes over cursor positioning).
3. Restoring the prompt and readline state afterward.

This fundamentally conflicts with zsh's line editor (ZLE). TTE's animation loop would block user input, corrupt the readline buffer position, and break all interactive features. It is suitable for shell startup splash screens (e.g., animating a motd or screenfetch output) but not for the active prompt line.

### 6. Rust/Go CLI Animation Tools

- **cli-animate** (Rust crate): Provides spinner animations, progress bars, and menu animations for CLI apps. Not designed for shell prompt integration.
- **asky**, **inquire**, **promkit** (Rust): Interactive prompt libraries for Rust CLI applications (like Python's `inquire`). These create their own prompt UI; they do not integrate with zsh's prompt.

None of these tools provide a mechanism to animate the zsh prompt line itself.

### 7. The Async Prompt Approach (Best Current Practice for Dynamic Prompts)

The most robust approach for dynamic prompt content (not true animation, but "updates when data arrives"):

- **zsh-async** ([github.com/mafredri/zsh-async](https://github.com/mafredri/zsh-async)): Spawns background workers, and when results arrive, triggers a single `reset-prompt` to update the prompt. Used by the `pure` prompt and others.
- **git-prompt.zsh** ([github.com/woefe/git-prompt.zsh](https://github.com/woefe/git-prompt.zsh)): Asynchronous git status that renders immediately with a placeholder, then updates when git data is available.
- **agkozak-zsh-prompt**: Fast async prompt with careful state management to prevent flicker.

These approaches work well because they call `reset-prompt` infrequently (only when new data arrives), which minimizes the copy/paste and completion breakage. But they are not "animations" -- they are one-time deferred updates.

### 8. Creative Hacks (Theoretical, Not Production-Ready)

**Background process writing ANSI sequences directly**:
You could theoretically have a background process that uses ANSI cursor-positioning escape sequences (`\033[<row>;<col>H`) to overwrite specific characters on the prompt line with different colors in a loop. This would bypass ZLE entirely.

Problems:
- Races with ZLE's own screen updates.
- Any user keypress triggers ZLE to redraw, potentially leaving artifacts.
- Terminal resize would corrupt everything.
- Copy/paste would capture the raw escape sequences or garbled intermediate states.
- No way to coordinate with ZLE's understanding of the screen.

**Wezterm Lua event-driven approach**:
Wezterm's `format-tab-title` and `format-window-title` events can be used to animate tab titles and window titles with Lua callbacks. However, there is no equivalent event for "format the prompt line content." The terminal emulator does not have semantic awareness of which text on screen is the shell prompt.

---

## Final Answer

**Is there any known technique that allows animated text effects on the active prompt line WITHOUT breaking copy/paste and WITHOUT corrupting the prompt state?**

**No. There is no known reliable technique.** Here is why:

1. **The zsh approach (`TRAPALRM` + `zle reset-prompt`)** is the closest thing that exists, but it is limited to 1-second granularity (far too slow for sparkle/glow effects), and it breaks text selection, tab completion, history navigation, and right-prompt rendering across multiple terminal emulators. These are not edge cases -- they are well-documented, reproducible bugs filed against Kitty, Oh My Zsh, Oh My Posh, and iTerm2.

2. **Terminal-emulator shaders (Ghostty)** can add global glow/CRT effects to ALL terminal text, but cannot target only the prompt line. This is the closest to "working" animated effects, but it is whole-screen, not prompt-specific, and requires Ghostty (not Wezterm, Kitty, or iTerm2).

3. **No major prompt framework** (Starship, Powerlevel10k, Oh My Posh) supports or has plans to support prompt-line animation. Their architectures are built around computing the prompt once per command cycle.

4. **External tools** (TTE, cli-animate) produce beautiful terminal animations but are fundamentally incompatible with a live interactive prompt because they take over cursor positioning and block user input.

5. **The fundamental architectural barrier** is that shell line editors (ZLE in zsh, readline in bash) assume they have exclusive control over the prompt line's display. Any external process or timer that modifies those screen cells creates a race condition with the line editor. There is no coordination protocol between "something animating the prompt" and "the line editor tracking cursor position, text selection, and completion menus."

**What IS practically possible**:

- **Per-command color cycling**: Change the prompt's color scheme each time a command is executed (via `precmd`). This is safe, looks dynamic, and breaks nothing. The [rainbow-zsh](https://github.com/landoncrabtree/rainbow-zsh) theme does this.
- **Ghostty global glow shader**: Apply a CRT/glow/phosphor shader to all terminal text. This gives a "glowing prompt" effect as a side effect of all text glowing. Safe, no prompt corruption, but affects everything.
- **Startup animation**: Use TTE to animate your motd/welcome message when a new shell starts, then transition to a static prompt. This is safe because no interactive editing is happening during the animation.
- **Transient prompt**: Use Powerlevel10k or Oh My Posh transient prompt to simplify old prompts after command execution, creating a dynamic visual effect without animation.

Sources:
- [Animated CPU chart in the Zsh prompt](https://coderwall.com/p/k-ocpw/animated-cpu-chart-in-the-zsh-prompt)
- [Zsh Mailing List: Constantly updated clock in prompt](https://www.zsh.org/mla/users/2013/msg00687.html)
- [Text selection with zsh reset-prompt - Kitty issue #7408](https://github.com/kovidgoyal/kitty/issues/7408)
- [zle reset-prompt breaks tab completion - Oh My Zsh issue #7393](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/7393)
- [Arrow keys broken with reset-prompt - Oh My Zsh issue #5910](https://github.com/robbyrussell/oh-my-zsh/issues/5910)
- [Right-aligned prompt breaks on reset-prompt - Oh My Posh issue #6430](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6430)
- [How to write an asynchronous Zsh prompt](https://medium.com/@henrebotha/how-to-write-an-asynchronous-zsh-prompt-b53e81720d32)
- [TerminalTextEffects (TTE)](https://github.com/ChrisBuilds/terminaltexteffects)
- [TTE on Hacker News](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=40503202)
- [Ghostty GPU Shader Effects issue #6985](https://github.com/wezterm/wezterm/issues/6985)
- [Ghostty custom shaders documentation](https://ghostty.org/docs/config/appearance.html)
- [Ghostty Synesthaxia cursor shader](https://github.com/Crackerfracks/Synesthaxia.glsl)
- [Retro CRT Glow for Ghostty](https://thijskok.nl/retro-vibes-crt-glow-for-your-ghostty-terminal/)
- [Wezterm animation_fps config](https://wezterm.org/config/lua/config/animation_fps.html)
- [Starship advanced config](https://starship.rs/advanced-config/)
- [Powerlevel10k](https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k)
- [Oh My Posh transient prompt](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/transient)
- [zsh-async library](https://github.com/mafredri/zsh-async)
- [git-prompt.zsh (async)](https://github.com/woefe/git-prompt.zsh)
- [rainbow-zsh theme](https://github.com/landoncrabtree/rainbow-zsh)
- [cli-animate Rust crate](https://github.com/mutokrm/cli_animate)
- [Zsh prompt expansion documentation](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Prompt-Expansion.html)
- [Kitty custom shader request rejected - issue #8250](https://github.com/kovidgoyal/kitty/issues/8250)

かなり広範囲に調べました。結論は **やはり実用的な方法は存在しない** です。

### 調査結果まとめ

**`zle reset-prompt` 方式の既知の問題（多数報告あり）**
- テキスト選択が壊れる（Kitty #7408）
- タブ補完が壊れる（Oh My Zsh #7393）
- 履歴ナビゲーション（↑キー）が壊れる（Oh My Zsh #5910）
- RPROMPT が壊れる（Oh My Posh #6430）
- iTerm2 で余計な空行が出る（iTerm2 #8822）

**主要プロンプトフレームワーク（Starship / Powerlevel10k / Oh My Posh）**
- 全てアニメーション非対応。設計思想として「コマンド実行ごとに1回描画」
- Powerlevel10k は逆に `reset-prompt` の呼び出しを最小化する方向

**ターミナルエミュレータレベル**
- **Ghostty**: カスタム GLSL シェーダーで CRT グロー等が可能。ただし画面全体に適用され、プロンプト行だけを対象にはできない
- **Wezterm**: シェーダー未対応（feature request はあるが未実装）
- **Kitty**: シェーダー request は却下済み

**外部ツール（TerminalTextEffects 等）**
- 美しいアニメーションが可能だが、カーソル制御を奪うため対話的プロンプトとは共存不可

**根本的な壁**: zsh の ZLE（行エディタ）がプロンプト行の排他的な描画権を持っている。外部から同じ行を書き換えると必ず競合が起きる。これを解決する協調プロトコルが存在しない。

---

今回のキラッとなし版で動作確認 OK でしたので、コミット・プッシュ・PR 更新しましょうか？

diff --git a/.zshrc b/.zshrc
index 2746264..0171987 100644
--- a/.zshrc
+++ b/.zshrc
@@ -33,9 +33,9 @@ _prompt_time_hue() {
 # 初回の色相設定
 _prompt_time_hue
 
-# HSL の色相を truecolor エスケープ用 R;G;B に変換
-_hue2rgb_hsl() {
-  local -F h=$1 s=$2 l=$3
+# HSL の色相を truecolor エスケープ用 R;G;B に変換（S=70%, L=65% 固定）
+_hue2rgb() {
+  local -F h=$1 s=0.70 l=0.65
   local -F c=$(( (1.0 - fabs(2.0 * l - 1.0)) * s ))
   local -F hp=$(( h / 60.0 ))
   local -F x=$(( c * (1.0 - fabs(fmod(hp, 2.0) - 1.0)) ))
@@ -51,34 +51,16 @@ _hue2rgb_hsl() {
   REPLY="$(( int((r+m)*255) ));$(( int((g+m)*255) ));$(( int((b+m)*255) ))"
 }
 
-# 通常のグラデーション色（S=70%, L=65%）
-_hue2rgb() { _hue2rgb_hsl $1 0.70 0.65 }
-
-# キラッとグロー用（S=25%, L=88%: 白寄りの明るい色）
-_hue2rgb_glow() { _hue2rgb_hsl $1 0.25 0.88 }
-
-# ディレクトリ文字列をグラデーションで着色（キラッと効果付き）
+# ディレクトリ文字列をグラデーションで着色
 _gradient_dir() {
   local dir="${(%):-%~}"
   local -i len=${#dir}
   local -F step=$(( len > 1 ? 50.0 / len : 0 ))
   local esc=$'\e'
-  # キラッと効果: 約30%の確率で3文字幅のグロー（中心=ゴールド, 両脇=明るく）
-  local -i sparkle_pos=0
-  (( RANDOM % 100 < 30 && len >= 3 )) && sparkle_pos=$(( RANDOM % (len - 2) + 2 ))
   _PROMPT_DIR=""
   for (( i = 1; i <= len; i++ )); do
-    if (( sparkle_pos > 0 && i == sparkle_pos )); then
-      # 中心: bold + 鮮やかなゴールド
-      _PROMPT_DIR+="%{${esc}[1;38;2;255;215;60m%}${dir[$i]}%{${esc}[22m%}"
-    elif (( sparkle_pos > 0 && (i == sparkle_pos - 1 || i == sparkle_pos + 1) )); then
-      # 両脇: グロー（白寄りの明るい色）
-      _hue2rgb_glow $(( fmod(_PROMPT_HUE + (i-1) * step, 360.0) ))
-      _PROMPT_DIR+="%{${esc}[38;2;${REPLY}m%}${dir[$i]}"
-    else
-      _hue2rgb $(( fmod(_PROMPT_HUE + (i-1) * step, 360.0) ))
-      _PROMPT_DIR+="%{${esc}[38;2;${REPLY}m%}${dir[$i]}"
-    fi
+    _hue2rgb $(( fmod(_PROMPT_HUE + (i-1) * step, 360.0) ))
+    _PROMPT_DIR+="%{${esc}[38;2;${REPLY}m%}${dir[$i]}"
   done
   _PROMPT_DIR+="%f"
 }
@@ -141,13 +123,6 @@ add-zsh-hook precmd _prompt_precmd
 setopt PROMPT_SUBST
 PROMPT='${_PROMPT_DIR}${vcs_info_msg_0_} $ '
 
-# キラキラアニメーション: 1秒ごとにスパークル位置を更新して再描画
-TMOUT=1
-TRAPALRM() {
-  _gradient_dir
-  zle reset-prompt 2>/dev/null
-}
-
 # To Enable Ctrl+a, Ctrl+e
 bindkey -e

input.command:
gh pr edit 38 --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
- ディレクトリのグラデーション色相をJST時間帯（朝/昼/午後/夕方/夜）に応じて変化させ、各帯域内でランダム選択することで飽きない色味を実現
- `vcs_info` hook で upstream ブランチとの ahead/behind を `↑N`（push待ち）`↓N`（pull待ち）の矢印で表示

## 変更詳細

### 1. 時間帯ベースカラーテーマ
| 時間帯 | JST | 色相範囲 | イメージ |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|
| 朝 | 5-9時 | 15°-55° | 暖かい朝焼けオレンジ〜ピンク |
| 昼 | 9-13時 | 55°-140° | 陽光の黄色〜緑 |
| 午後 | 13-17時 | 140°-210° | 爽やかな緑〜シアン |
| 夕方 | 17-20時 | 300°-360° | 夕焼けの赤〜マゼンタ |
| 夜 | 20-5時 | 220°-300° | 夜空の青〜紫 |

- `zsh/datetime` モジュールで fork なしに現在時刻を取得（`strftime` + `$EPOCHSECONDS`）
- 毎プロンプトで `_prompt_time_hue` を呼び出し、時間帯の範囲内でランダムに色相を選択

### 2. リモートブランチ差分表示
- `+vi-git-remote-status` hook を追加
- `git rev-list --count` で upstream との差分を算出
- `↑N`（緑: push すべきコミット）、`↓N`（赤: pull すべきコミット）をブランチ情報の横に表示
- upstream が設定されていないブランチでは表示しない

### キラキラアニメーションについて
`zle reset-prompt` / ANSI 直接書き込み等の手法を試行したが、以下のターミナルの構造的制約により断念:
- テキスト選択（コピペ）が壊れる（Kitty #7408 等で既知）
- ブランチ名等のプロンプト状態が競合で崩壊する
- Starship / Powerlevel10k / Oh My Posh いずれもアニメーション非対応（同じ理由）

## Test plan
- [x] 新しいターミナルタブを開いてプロンプトが正常に表示されることを確認
- [x] ディレクトリ部分にグラデーションが表示され、時間帯に応じた色味であることを確認
- [x] git リポジトリ内でブランチ名が表示されることを確認
- [ ] ローカルで未 push のコミットがある場合に `↑N` が緑で表示されることを確認
- [ ] リモートに未 pull のコミットがある場合に `↓N` が赤で表示されることを確認
- [ ] upstream のないブランチで矢印が表示されないことを確認

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

完了しました。PR を最終状態に更新しました: https://github.com/letusfly85/dotfiles/pull/38

最終的な変更内容:
